cards.back: Private IP of the inside host as seen from inside the network (e.g., 10.0.0.5).

cards.front: Private IP of the inside host as seen from inside the network (e.g., 10.0.0.5).

cards.back: Public IP of the inside host as seen from the outside network — what the world sees as the source.

cards.front: Public IP of the inside host as seen from the outside network — what the world sees as the source.

cards.back: IP of the outside host as seen from inside the network (usually same as Outside Global unless dual NAT).

cards.front: IP of the outside host as seen from inside the network (usually same as Outside Global unless dual NAT).

cards.back: Real public IP of the outside host on the internet.

cards.front: Real public IP of the outside host on the internet.

cards.back: Which side of the NAT router the host lives on (inside = your LAN, outside = the internet).

cards.front: Which side of the NAT router the host lives on (inside = your LAN, outside = the internet).

cards.back: Which side the address is meaningful on (local = inside network's view, global = outside network's view).

cards.front: Which side the address is meaningful on (local = inside network's view, global = outside network's view).

cards.back: One-to-one fixed mapping between an inside local and inside global address.

cards.front: One-to-one fixed mapping between an inside local and inside global address.

cards.back: Many-to-many mapping from a pool — inside locals get an available inside global from the pool.

cards.front: Many-to-many mapping from a pool — inside locals get an available inside global from the pool.

cards.back: Many-to-one mapping using port numbers to distinguish flows. Most common NAT in practice.

cards.front: Many-to-one mapping using port numbers to distinguish flows. Most common NAT in practice.
Which NAT type is also called "NAT Overload"?
In PAT, what distinguishes multiple inside hosts sharing one public IP?
cards.back: Source port numbers — the NAT router rewrites the source port to a unique value per flow and tracks the mapping.

cards.front: Mark an interface as the inside NAT interface

cards.back: Mark an interface as the inside NAT interface

cards.front: Mark an interface as the outside NAT interface

cards.back: Mark an interface as the outside NAT interface

cards.front: Configure PAT using the outside interface IP
cards.back:
```
ip nat inside source list 1 interface Gi0/1 overload
access-list 1 permit 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255
```

cards.front: If a host's traffic hits NAT but `show ip nat translations` is empty, what is usually wrong?
cards.back: The `ip nat inside` / `ip nat outside` interface markings are missing or swapped — the router won't create translations without them.
